flashcards.Back: The heart chamber that receives deoxygenated blood from the systemic circulation via the superior and inferior vena cava.
The thickest heart chamber that pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body through the aorta.
The only artery in the human body that carries deoxygenated blood, moving it from the right ventricle to the lungs.
The only vein in the human body that carries oxygenated blood, moving it from the lungs to the left atrium.
The heart valve located between the right atrium and the right ventricle that prevents backflow during contraction.
The primary pacemaker of the heart located in the right atrium, typically firing at a rate of $60-100$ beats per minute.
The secondary pacemaker that creates a slight delay in the electrical signal to allow the ventricles to fill with blood.
The specialized conduction fibers that distribute the electrical impulse rapidly throughout the ventricular myocardium.
The pressure in the filled ventricles at the end of diastole, representing the amount of blood returning to the heart.
The systemic vascular resistance the left ventricle must pump against to eject blood into the aorta.
The formula for Cardiac Output, which is the volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute.
Chest pain resulting from myocardial ischemia that does not cause cell death and is typically relieved by rest or nitroglycerin.
The death of heart muscle tissue due to prolonged ischemia, often caused by a blockage in a coronary artery.
An AMI that presents with atypical symptoms like weakness or nausea instead of chest pain, common in diabetics and the elderly.
Failure that causes blood to back up into the lungs, resulting in pulmonary edema, rales, and orthopnea.
Failure that causes blood to back up into the systemic circulation, resulting in jugular venous distention and pedal edema.
Fluid accumulation in the lowest part of the body, such as the feet and ankles, often signifying right heart failure.
A life-threatening accumulation of fluid in the lungs that often produces pink, frothy sputum.
An anti-platelet medication that prevents platelets from clumping together to form or enlarge blood clots.
The standard EMT dose is $162mg$ to $324mg$ administered as chewed baby aspirin.
A vasodilator that relaxes smooth muscle in blood vessels, decreasing preload and reducing the workload of the heart.
One sublingual dose of $0.4mg$ every $5$ minutes as needed, up to a maximum of $3$ doses.
Nitroglycerin should not be administered if the patient's systolic blood pressure is less than $100$ $mmHg$.
Do not give nitroglycerin if the patient has taken erectile dysfunction medication within the last $24-48$ hours.
A chaotic and lethal heart rhythm with no organized electrical activity, resulting in no pulse.
A rapid heart rhythm originating in the ventricles that can be pulseless and is a shockable rhythm for an AED.
The complete absence of all electrical and mechanical activity in the heart, appearing as a flat line on a monitor.
A condition where the heart's electrical system appears normal on a monitor, but the muscle does not contract or produce a pulse.
The prompt to immediately resume high-quality chest compressions and continue the cycle of CPR.
The delivery of an electric shock to the heart to stop lethal dysrhythmias and allow the SA node to regain control.
The five essential steps to improve cardiac arrest survival: early access, early CPR, early defibrillation, early ALS, and integrated post-arrest care.
The Return of Spontaneous Circulation, identified by a palpable pulse and potentially spontaneous breathing after cardiac arrest.
A consistent rate of $100-120$ compressions per minute for adults, children, and infants.
Chest compressions should be at least $2$ inches ($5$ $cm$) deep but no more than $2.4$ inches ($6$ $cm$).
A compression-to-ventilation ratio of $30:2$ for both single and two-rescuer adult CPR.
Compressions should be approximately $2$ inches deep or one-third the total depth of the chest.
Compressions should be $1.5$ inches deep or one-third the total depth of the infant's chest.
The compression-to-ventilation ratio for children and infants changes to $15:2$ when two rescuers are present.
The primary pulse point used to assess circulation in an unresponsive infant.
The primary pulse point used to assess circulation in an unresponsive adult or child.
One breath every $6$ seconds ($10$ breaths per minute) if the patient has a pulse but is not breathing.
One breath every $2-3$ seconds ($20-30$ breaths per minute) if the child or infant has a pulse but is not breathing.
A condition where inner layers of the aorta tear, causing sudden, severe, tearing pain between the shoulder blades.
A severe elevation in blood pressure, usually above $180/120$ $mmHg$, that may cause headache, nausea, or altered mental status.
Severe respiratory distress triggered by lying in a flat position, commonly seen in congestive heart failure patients.
Manually displace the uterus to the left side during CPR to prevent vena cava compression and improve blood return.
A temporary deficiency in blood supply and oxygen to a tissue or organ, often causing pain.
The permanent death of tissue resulting from a lack of blood supply, as seen in a heart attack.
Standard placement is on the upper right chest and the lower left side of the chest below the nipple line.
Administer oxygen to a chest pain patient only if their $SpO2$ is less than $94\%$ or they show signs of respiratory distress.
